Sustainable development
02
Pollution prevention and control – other activities
The low-emission vehicles that Renault Group sells,
finances, leases or operates are all equipped with tyres in
external road noise and resistance classes that comply with
the European requirements set out in EC Regulation 661/
2009. As the requirements of the Taxonomy go beyond
regulatory compliance on this criterion, an additional
analysis was conducted and demonstrated that most tyre
references originally fitted to low-emission vehicles meet
this criterion. However, despite the Group’s best efforts, it
was not possible to verify this for all the financed vehicles, as
information on their actual tyre fitment is not available. To
date, this criterion is considered inoperable. This position will
be reassessed in the future depending on the availability of
the necessary data.
With a certified noise level well below 68 dBA, Renault
electric vehicles have met, since 2021, the external noise
limits applicable starting in 2024, thus contributing to the
reduction of ambient noise and the quality of life in urban
areas. All low-emission vehicles placed on the market by
Renault Group in Europe in 2024 therefore comply with
European regulation 540/2014/EC applicable to vehicles
certified since July 2016, which require a maximum of 72 dBA.
Renault Group’s dismantling facilities are equipped to
manage and store safely and in an environmentally sound
manner hazardous substances, mixtures and components
removed during the depollution operations, and comply with
European regulation.
Protection and restoration of biodiversity and
ecosystems – all activities
In addition to the environmental impact studies conducted
in accordance with national and European regulations,
Renault Group is proactive in preserving biodiversity and
ecosystems (See chapter 2.2.2.4).
2.2.2.6.1.3 Verification of compliance with the
minimum safeguards
These minimum guarantees cover four main areas:
●Corruption and bribery: policies, actions and results are
outlined in chapter 2.2.4.1.2;
●Competition: policies, actions and results are set out in
chapter 2.2.4.1.7;
●Human rights: policies, actions and results are set out in
chapters 2.2.3.1.6 and 2.2.3.2;
●Tax:
Renault
Group’s
Tax
Department
ensures
compliance with the tax rules applicable to its activity in
all countries where the Group operates, in accordance
with international conventions and local laws, through an
appropriate management system.
To the best of its knowledge, Renault Group was not convicted
in 2024 for corruption, tax evasion, or human rights violations.
 Renault Group    I    Universal registration document 2024
175

2.2.3 Social Information
2.2.3.1 Own workforce (ESRS S1)

likelihood, made without taking existing prevention and mitigation policies and actions into account (gross assessment).

## Page 184

Renault Group
Sustainable development
2.2.3.1.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts,
Risks and Opportunities and
their interaction with strategy
and business model – transversal
Renault Group recognizes the diverse nature of its
workforce which includes employees and non-employees,
described below as workers.
The Group defines employees as all workers having an
employment contract (permanent and fixed-term contracts)
at the end of the reporting period within a consolidated entity
of the Group. Non-employees are temporary workers and
workers with no employment contract such as interns in
France (internship contract) or VIE (volunteer for international
experience). [DP S1.SBM-3_02]
2.2.3.1.2 S1-3 – Processes to prevent, mitigate
and remediate negative impacts
and channels for own workforce
to raise concerns – transversal
Renault Group has deployed a whistleblowing system,
WhistlB, which is a confidential and secure platform for
collecting reports, open to all employees (including
individuals seeking employment and former employees),
who can report facts contrary to the law, the Code of Ethics,
or the Anti-corruption Code of Conduct.
The WhistleB system is complemented by the regular
internal channels for reporting whistleblowing, which
include
management,
Human
Resources,
employee
representatives, the Ethics & Compliance Department and
the network of Ethics & Compliance officers.
For more information on WhistleB and the management of
whistleblowers, see chapter 2.2.4.1.2.2. [DP S1-3_01; DP S1-
3_02; DP S1-3_05 to 09]
2.2.3.1.3 Working conditions
and environment
2.2.3.1.3.1 SBM-3 – Material Impacts, Risks
and Opportunities and their interaction
with strategy and business model
A. Working conditions and human rights
Ethics and sustainable development are central to Renault
Group’s strategic vision. The organization, both as an employer
and as an automotive manufacturer, is committed to
achieving excellence in the respect and promotion of human
rights. Renault Group ensures that its Purpose and values,
which are deeply ingrained among its workforce, are reflected
in its business practices. This alignment fosters a constructive
environment in the regions where it operates and strengthens
relationships with its partners. Furthermore, Renault Group
places a high priority on the capacity of its operational entities
to swiftly address and rectify any potential infringements on
human rights or fundamental freedoms that may arise from its
commercial activities.
Renault Group acknowledges the material risks on its activity
arising from the potential impacts of human and labor rights
infringements on its own workforce. One key risk involves
potential remediation costs, penalties, and reputational
damage from non-compliance with working conditions
regulations and human rights, which can negatively affect
workers’ psychological and physical well-being. Additionally,
poor working conditions can lead to a loss of workforce
productivity, as they can have adverse psychological and
physical impacts on employees. [DP S1.SBM-3_05]
Renault Group is committed to enhancing the working
conditions that contribute to the psychological and physical
well-being of all its workers. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-3_12]
Renault Group has developed an understanding of the
widespread impacts that may be faced by its workforce
through a comprehensive approach to human rights and
working conditions. However, Renault Group considers that,
except for specificities related to the type of task performed,
all individuals within its workforce are considered at equal risk
of harm. [DP S1.SBM-3_03; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group has not identified any activity or geography
that presents a particularly high risk in terms of forced labor
or child labor. [DP S1.SBM-3_07 to 10]
B. Health and safety
Renault Group has a responsibility to protect its employees
by maintaining a secure workplace that prevents accidents
and work-related illnesses. Given the nature of its
operations, the types of tasks performed, and the materials
handled by its workforce, Renault Group faces numerous
health and safety hazards that could be harmful to its
employees and all individuals working on Renault Group
sites. Due to the nature of its activities, material negative
impacts are mostly related to individual incidents.  Renault
Group is committed to offering a work environment that not
only fulfills legal requirements but also establishes
exemplary benchmarks in HSEE (Health, Safety, Ergonomics
& Environment), guided by dedicated Mandatory Rules (MR)
related to risk prevention, which includes, for example, the
principles of the 5S methodology. The 5S methodology is a
workplace organization system focused on improving
efficiency, safety, and sustainability. It involves sorting,
organizing,
cleaning,
standardizing,
and
maintaining
discipline to enhance both productivity and workers’ well-
being. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-3_03]
Renault Group considers all individuals affected by its
activities equally, regardless of their gender, age, country,
etc., ensuring the same level of prevention and protection.
The Group acknowledges that all its employees, all
individuals working on its sites, as well as temporary
workers, could be materially impacted by the company’s
operations and value chain. [DP S1.SBM-3_01; DP S1.SBM-
3_02; DP S1.SBM-3_11]
Renault Group is aware of the risk of increased operational
expenditure due to absenteeism in the workplace. The Group
acknowledges that implementing an ambitious Health and
Safety policy does not only reduce costs (due to absenteeism,
turnover, legal actions, health monitoring, etc.) but also
increases
productivity,
employee
motivation,
and

Renault Group has proactively taken into consideration
electrical risks in all its activities related to engineering,
manufacturing, sale, and after-sale through its HSEE
Department. This includes a safe inherent design policy as
well as training and the granting of proper authorizations.
[DP S1.SBM-3_06]

2.2.3.1.3.2 S1-1 – Policies related to own workforce
A. Working conditions and human rights
Renault Group’s human rights policy is aligned with key
documents such as the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human
Rights, the UN Global Compact principles, and the OECD
Guidelines
for
Multinational
Enterprises.
The
Group’s
commitment to ethical labor practices is demonstrated by its
adherence to the 2013 “Committing together for sustainable
growth and development” Global Framework Agreement
(GFA), the 2019 GFA on “Working together to build a Renault
Group working environment”, and to the International Labour
Organization (ILO) Conventions, ensuring the elimination of
forced labor and setting the minimum working age at 15, in
compliance with local laws.
For more information on Renault Group’s engagement with
its workforce, see chapter 2.2.3.1.5. [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP
S1-1_04; DP S1-1_07; DP S1-1_08; DP MDR-P_01; DP MDR-
P_02; DP MDR-P_04]
The Group’s human rights commitments include:
●Prohibition of child labor in accordance with ILO’s Minimum
Age Convention, 1973 (no. 138), ILO’s Worst Forms of Child
Labour Convention, 1999 (no. 182) and the 2013 and 2019 GFAs;
●Prohibition of forced labor in accordance with ILO’s
Forced Labour Convention, 1930 (no. 29), ILO’s Abolition of
Forced Labour Convention, 1957 (no. 105), and the 2013 and
2019 GFAs;
●A no harassment, violence, or retaliation policy in
accordance with Article 5 of the Universal Declaration of
Human Rights, the 2019 GFA and the Renault Group
Diversity and Inclusion Charter;
●Freedom of association, dialogue and consultation with
employees, effective recognition of the right to collective
bargaining in accordance with ILO’s Freedom of
Association and Protection of the Right to Organize
Convention, 1948 (No. 87), ILO’s Right to Organize and
Collective Bargaining Convention, 1949 (no. 98), ILO’s
Workers’ Representatives Convention, 1971 (no. 135) aiming
to prevent any form of discrimination on the grounds of
trade union involvement, and with the 2013 and 2019 GFAs
(including the 2019 GFA’s 2021 addendum). [DP S1-1_04;
DP S1-1_07; DP MDR-P_04]
Moreover, by endorsing the United Nations Women’s
Empowerment Principles (WEP), Renault Group reaffirms
that the Universal Declaration of Human Rights must apply
equally to women and men.  [DP S1.MDR-P_01-06; DP S1-
1_07; DP MDR-P_02]
Renault Group adheres to ILO’s Convention no. 100,
advocating for equal pay for work of equal value. The Group
ensures that all its employees are paid an adequate wage.
[DP S1-10_01; DP MDR-P_04]
Renault Group fosters a culture of open dialogue between
management and workforce, facilitated by its Group Works
Council at a global level and local employee representative
bodies. The Group commits to international norms and
standards aimed at supporting or regulating corporate
environmental, social, and societal practices. This adherence
to international principles and standards for the protection of
human rights is also enshrined in the 2013 and 2019 GFAs.
Renault
Group
has
signed
various
agreements
(See
chapter 2.2.3.1.5) that emphasize the importance of balancing
professional
and
personal
life,
offering
flexible
work
arrangements such as remote working, fostering inclusion, and
implementing progressive management styles.
The
Group
diligently
ensures
effective
employee
representation across its entities in accordance with local
legislation
through
elected
or
union-affiliated
staff
members, reflecting the organisation’s dedication to its
workforce’s well-being and rights.  [DP S1-1_05; DP MDR-
P_01; DP MDR-P_02; DP MDR-P_04; DP MDR-P_05]
The Group Works Council plays a pivotal role in representing
employees
globally.
The
Chief
People,
Workplace,
Organisation Officer represents the most senior level within
Renault Group accountable for the implementation of the
working conditions and human rights policies. [DP MDR-P_03]
Renault Group addresses psychological and physical
impacts on workers through its human rights governance
framework. Risk mapping identifies significant human rights
risks, and ensures that, as the case may be, corrective
actions are implemented to mitigate negative impacts and
enhance workers’ well-being. [DP S1-1_06]
The Group’s policies are publicly available on its website and
communicated internally via its intranet, regular meetings,
and training sessions. [DP MDR-P_06]
